sentences.text: You couldn't give me a prettier face to look at.
It's Sonia-- excuse me-- Shavah Konsens.
And I think I gave you the spelling.
K-O-N-S-E-N-S.
I was born in Belchatow, Poland, in 2/20/22.
My father's name was Moshe Konsons, and my mother's name was Reisel Rachel Konsons.
My father had a-- like a bleach factory-- or dye factory.
And should I explain what they did?
They-- cotton that, you know-- Poland-- especially large-- in Belchatow was a big textile industry.
And this was-- the cotton, when it came, it came in big-- I don't know how to describe it-- like, not bowls, but--
Yeah.
And then this had to be-- and it was like the color of-- let's see-- like winter white.
And this had to be made white.
For whatever color you wanted to make, it had to be white first.
And then you could make it into blue, or red, or black or whatever.
But it had to be white first.
So that what my father did.
And then that-- this went to different factories, and that's what the-- his-- was his-- his occupation.
My mother had three children, and she didn't have a special occupation.
At home, my mother helped her parents.
They had a bakery.
And when she got married, her sisters took her job.
[LAUGHS] But she didn't work outside of the home.
My sister's name was [NON-ENGLISH] Konsons.
My brother's name was [NON-ENGLISH] Herschel Konsons.
My sister was born about 1925, and my brother about '28.
Well, my mother was the oldest of four children.
And she had a brother and two younger sisters.
I was the oldest granddaughter.
And I was-- I loved to spend time with my aunts and uncles.
Most of the time, a lot of the time, I spent when my sister and brother were born-- to-- I spend with my grandparents a lot of times, so things would be easier for my mother.
And I grew up in a very loving home.
My aunts, I adored them, and they adored me, because we weren't that much different in age.
And I spun-- spend a lot of time there.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] let's see.
My-- we spent a lot of time with my grandparents, let's say, holidays.
Not Friday nights, but Saturday afternoons we spent together.
The whole family used to come together in my grandparents' house, and they had a bakery.
And they-- we all got together like in the store and outside.
Everybody used to sit around and talk, and have all kinds of food that my grandmother prepared.
And besides the immediate family, there were also like nieces, and nephews, and cousins that used to come and gather there.
And I lost everybody.
I'm the only one that survived.
The first sign of trouble was 1939, the 1st of September.
All of a sudden, when we got up in the morning, there was bombing.
We heard planes coming over our city, because it was not too far from the German border.
And they-- you know, bombs were just flying.
And we grabbed practically-- my mother and I, and my-- and my sister, and brother, we just ran.
We just grabbed-- I don't know-- shoes and a coat, or whatever.
And we just ran.
We looked around, and we didn't see my father.
And-- and the whole city-- everybody ran.
And we ran to the next town, which was-- I don't know how many kilometers-- maybe 14, 15 kilometers-- and-- till this [? kind of ?] bombing calm-- quieted down.
And we kept running there.
And we ran a whole day.
And then we came to some kind of a village when it was dark.
And the-- the owners of the farm put us up in a barn.
And I'll never forget the flies there.
It was just horrible.
And let's say, the next day, we wanted to go back.
But we couldn't go back, because the German soldiers were coming with all their tanks, and all their equipment, and marching.
We couldn't go back.
It took about a week till we finally were able to go back to our city.
All this time we didn't hear about my father, and we thought for sure maybe he got killed or something happened to him.
On the way home-- this was about a week later-- so we-- on the way home, we saw a-- a woman that knew my mother.
She was a gentile woman.
And she told my mother that my father is alive, that she saw him.
So we came home.
And we came in front of our house, and it was burned.
Everything was burned to the ground-- the-- like half that street was burned to the ground.
And we went to my grandfather's house.
My father was there, and we stayed with my grandfather.
At that time was the first time I saw my father after that week.
And he was, at that time, the-- the-- a law was-- came out that everybody had to shave off the beards, and you know.
So I saw my father the first time without a beard and my grandfather without a beard.
And I will never forget the impression I had when I saw my grandfather.
He was, at that time, maybe 70, and his-- had wrinkles on his face.
And my father looked so young.
I couldn't believe that's my father, because he had a beard, and they took off his beard.
He looked-- with blue eyes, he just looked like a-- a [LAUGHS] gentile man, you know, not like my father.
And it took me, for the longest time, just-- I just kept looking at him.
And I couldn't believe this is my father.
Anyway, since we didn't-- our house was burned down, but you know, they were talking a few weeks about the war, and they're gonna bomb, and this, and that.
So my parents hid some things in the cellar.
And since my father was there, he was able to take-- rescue all these things that were in the cellar.
And he brought it to my grandparents, to the bakery.
And that's where we stayed for a while.
And things got very hard.
You know, all-- every day there were new-- new laws and regulations coming out, and we had to just obey by these.
In the beginning, my grandparents, they were able to operate the bakery, just for a few weeks maybe.
And so my father help-- helped them, because his parents were also in the bakery.
But my-- when I-- when my mother and father got married, both my-- my father's parents were dead.
So I never knew them.
But he grew up also in a bakery, and he knew, I guess, what-- you know, how to do everything.
So he helped my grandfather with the bakery, because the employees that they-- and my-- no.
My uncle wasn't there.
My uncle was-- he came back later.
So my father helped out.
And they, you know, were able to bake things and sell the bread.
And things were going like halfway normal.
Then there was like a curfew.
We couldn't walk, let's say, in the street after 6 o'clock or in-- and then we couldn't walk on the side.
We had to walk in the-- in the gutter.
And every day there's new things came out.
We had to, let's say, there came a-- a ordinance that you had to give up all the gold.
If you had any gold, they catch you with the gold, they're gonna shoot you.
If you-- if you had any valuables, or silver whatever, or fur coats whatever, they-- everything they had to-- you had to give up, bring to a certain place.
And of course, everybody complied, because you didn't want to be shot.
And then-- then came out a law.
And then came out a-- my-- we stayed where there was the bakery.
There was like two rooms which my grandparents had that were like they-- because they would spend a lot of time there.
But they had a separate apartment someplace else where they lived.
And my-- after a while, my grandparents had to give up the apartment, because there was a German owner, and he took it back.
He didn't want them as tenants anymore, or he wasn't allowed to.
I don't know.
Anyway, so my grandparents had to move into this place where we stayed.
And the Jewish-- so we had no place to live.
So the Jewish community, you know, there were like empty stores.
Like there was the butcher shops that used to be empty or grocery stores that used to stay empty, because they closed them all up.
So they gave us an-- like a store that used to be a butcher shop.
And this was like our apartment where we lived-- just a little, little store.
Maybe it was as big as this here.
And it was like-- the-- the floors was made out of stone and was very cold.
And we had to put in some kind of a stove for cooking.
Our furniture, you know, burned down, but my grandparents gave us, you know, beds and all the-- whatever was necessary.
So we put in this store, and we lived there.
And slowly, slowly things got so bad.
My-- my father-- they closed my grandfather-- parents' bakery.
He wasn't allowed to work anymore.
And my grandfather had a friend that was-- had about four blocks away, a German friend, that he had a bakery also.
So my-- my grandfather talked to him and told him that, you know, my father needs a job, you know.
He has-- has to support his family.
So he took him in to work for a while.
Then came out a law that you were not-- you weren't allowed to employ Jews.
So my father practically-- he had nothing-- nothing to do to support his family.
And I was the oldest.
And I was, at that time, maybe 16, 17 years old.
So I kind of-- and then there was all kinds of laws.
And we were afraid my father shouldn't go out.
They shouldn't-- because they caught people to work, and they used to beat them.
And so we wanted my father to be as little as possible on the outside.
So I used to-- we had some people that they used to make socks.
You know, they had a little machine at home, and they used to make socks.
So I would take the socks and go from, you know, to some people that I knew to sell the socks, you know-- gentile people that I knew-- and sell the socks, and, you know, whatever I could.
Then, across the street from where my grandfather lived, there was the German church.
It was an Evangelical church.
And-- and this was right across from my grandfather's house and bakery-- practically from the bakery.
And so I went over to her, and I told her that she should give me any kind of work, that I have to help out and-- with the family things like-- my sister is younger.
My brother is younger.
And there's nobody to make any money.
So she gave me a job.
She gave me a job to work in the house.
So I used to help her with the cooking.
I remember-- I don't remember if they had children.
I think they had children, because they were so young.
And she really was very nice to me.
And some of the things that we used to have that my father took out from the cellar-- so let's say, one time we sold her the candlesticks.
One time we sold her, you know, because I was afraid.
Because he was supposed to give it up.
But we-- some things we kept.
So she gave us some money for this, some money for that.
Then I helped her.
She had like a big garden, a vegetable garden, in the back of the house.
So I used to, you know, help in the gardens.
I used to-- she used to give me vegetables, potatoes, and tomatoes, and cucumbers, all the things that are good with the beets, to take home.
And sometimes she used to entertain the German SS, and I would be in the next room.
You know, I really didn't look that Jewish.
And so I would work in the-- over there.
Sometimes I would do things.
And I would be scared to death, because I knew then in the next room there are the SS.
And she would also be in trouble if-- if-- [LAUGHS] you know, if they would catch me.
But they were really very nice, and so I worked there for awhile.
Then my father went-- you know, this was, let's say, maybe six, seven months into the war.
In the meantime, they did all kinds of-- they took away my uncles, you know, my mother's two-- my mother's brother.
My mother's brother actually was in the-- in the Polish army, and so in '39, they drafted him.
And he was fighting on the Russian front, and we didn't know if he's alive or dead.
But my uncle was married, and he had two children.
And his wife died when she gave birth to the little-- to her youngest son-- youngest boy.
They had two children.
So his mother-in-law took care of the children, and she lived about-- also in some kind of a store, you know, close to us and my grandparents.
And we used to take care of the children-- you know, my mother.
Because she was getting very elderly-- the-- the-- grandmother of the-- of the children.
And then my grandfather-- my uncle came back.
He came back, you know, because he said that he could have gone into Russia.
And everybody begged him not to go back, because they're gonna, you know, what's going on with the Germans.
But he said he has his children there, and his family is there.
And he has to go.
He has to go there to see what's going-- happening with them.
So he came back, and he was, you know, just dirty-- no shoes, you know, rags on his legs.
And he was really-- terrible shape.
So, you know-- but he came back.
After seven months he-- when he was home, they took-- took him.
My mother's, you know, the Germans took him away to a-- to a-- Posen, I think, at that time-- and my-- my mother's brother and my mother's brother-in-law.
So, you know, whenever-- whenever a man went out on the street, he was never safe.
They always caught him and dragged them off to work-- to send them away.
From my uncle, we never, never heard.
They-- he died there.
And my mother's two sisters, also, they were also both married.
And they each had two children.
One had two-- one little boy.
The older one, the one that-- next to my mother, she had two little girls.
And my-- and the youngest sister had a-- a little boy.
And then the-- and the other one was born-- she was pregnant while-- when the war broke out.
He was born in the war.
So her two sisters had-- each had children.
And I loved my little cousins, because I was the oldest.
I used to schlep them around.
And I just loved them.
And my-- my father, one time, he went out.
You know, he had friends, gentile people, that he used to know in different villages.
So he would go to them.
And-- and they used to give us food.
And he would come home with the food, and lasted for a while, you know, till we needed more.
And then they gave you like-- the Germans gave you like a rations.
Let's say, they gave you a bread, a loaf of bread, that was maybe two, three pounds for a whole week, for each person.
And you just had to, you know, kind of divide it up into the days.
And my mother, I remember that she used to cut up the loaf of bread, and this is for this day, and this is for this day, and this is for this one, for that.
And she just prayed to God.
And she says, God should help me.
I should live.
But there's gonna be a day that I won't have to count the pieces, or measure the piece of bread that I'm giving my children.
And it got progressively worse.
You couldn't, you know, you couldn't buy-- you couldn't get clothes.
You couldn't get anything.
They took everything away.
They took away when they came into, let's say, to a house-- mine-- my aunt had a beautiful house, and she, you know, she had the most beautiful furniture.
The SS came.
They took everything away-- all her furniture.
And then we had my mother's youngest sister, her husband was a tailor.
And he-- as a matter of fact, I have a picture.
This is the only picture I have from my family.
So he was working for the Germans.
So-- and they used to come up there.
And they didn't take away her furniture.
So she had her furniture.
And-- and so I used to help out my aunt with the children, you know, and they had a little more food.
And she had to help my grandparents.
And, you know, she had to kind of help everybody out a little bit.
And it was really a very, very hard time.
And then they started to, you know, catch girls for work, you know, to clean the SS.
The people from the SS, we had to clean their houses, scrub the floors, clean everything, clean their windows.
Sometimes I would stay on the third floor and clean a window.
And I thought, I'm just gonna fall down and just die, you know, there.
Sometimes I wished that would happen.
Because it was just hard to see how my parents suffer-- my father, and my mother, and my grandparents, especially.
And I remember a little incident.
My brother was, at that time, maybe 11 years old, 12 years old.
He wasn't bar mitzvah yet.

And he played in the yard-- next-- next yard-- with a-- with a little German boy-- that they used to always play together when-- whenever they were at the-- at the-- in the house from my grandparents.
Because we lived in a different street.
But during the war, we lived there.
So I don't know.
Apparently, he had a fight with this little German boy.
And the-- his-- the boy's mother, you know, wanted to-- he came home and told that my brother beat him up.
And they wanted to take my-- you know, they were looking for him.
And we hid him, you know, where my grandfather had this-- the bakery.
There was like a big shack that was for wood or coal.
So we hid my brother there.
And at that time they, took my father and my grandfather.
And they beat them up so much.
They held them for I don't know how many days.
And we went to the Jewish [ BACKROUND NOISE ] community, and, you know, to, you know, to-- and we-- I don't know how much money we had to give at that time to free my father and my-- my grandfather.
Because my brother [LAUGHS] you know, apparently beat up this little German boy.
And there is so many little incidents.
You know, like we had, where we used to live, the houses burned down.
Next door to us was a German-- German people that lived-- were our neighbors.
And we always had wonderful relations with them.
And sometimes I used to go there.
And they used to-- she used to give us food, you know, or she would bring sometimes things to our house.
But she was scared of her own children, that her children might turn her in-- that she's helping us.
So it was just the most horrendous thing just to live from day to day.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] My son just came in.
[SIGHS] And-- and at that time, when they-- we had to give all this money to bring my father.
And, you know, through the, you know, there in the Jewish commentate, always had these [NON-ENGLISH], you know, like they talk to the Germans.
And they, you know, for a price, they-- so they told us we had to give them so much money.
I don't know from where we took the money at that time, but somehow I think neighbors and everybody helped out.
And that took my father and my-- my grandfather back.
And from that time, they were really scared to go out in the street.
So my father just stayed in the house.
And I was the one that really-- and my brother and sister were much younger.
So they weren't of much help.
So I was the one that really worked and did whatever I could to help the family out.
And one day, this was-- this was about, let's see.
And one day, my father-- my father got sick.
And [PAUSES FOR4 SECONDS] this was in, I think in-- this was already shortly before-- before the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --anyway.
Mine-- my uncle was still there.
And they opened up a factory in our city where the temple, the [NON-ENGLISH] and the [NON-ENGLISH] used to be.
So they made a factory there where they made German uniforms for nurses, for the Germans.
And my uncle, had to-- you know, since he was a tailor, he and some-- and my cousins, and some other people, were working there.
So they-- and they paid them very little.
But in order not for them to be deported, so they worked, and they were like safe.
One day there was like-- my uncle came in the middle of the night.
And he said that they-- there were like, you know, all these rumors-- all these rumors going around.
This was maybe 1941.
And the rumors were that they were gonna take the girls away, you know, all young girls, that they're gonna take them away.
So my uncle, since he was in the factory at that time, he took me to his house.
And his house was like, you know, they-- he had a lot of connections with Germans, that he worked for them, made them suits, and things like this, for their wives.
So I-- they took me to my uncle's house.
And at-- at that time, you know, it-- it wasn't true.
They didn't take away girls.
But they made-- and they took away men.
So what-- whoever men they found, they knocked in the night-- in the middle of the night on the door.
Whatever men they found, they took 'em away.
And-- and from that time, on I-- I worked in the factory.
And I don't remember what they paid.
It was very minimal, but at-- if you worked, they-- you-- they gave you, let's say, more bread.
They gave you so much sugar a week, and so much, you know.
So it was a little, you know, kind of a little better.
And this went on like this.
You know, we tried to sell little things, whatever.
I remember we had the most beautiful bedspread.
You know, it was from velvet, and it was so gorgeous, you know, for two beds.
Because at home, we had two beds.
And when we-- this was kind of the last thing that we wanted to give up.
Because you're-- we're-- we're gonna survive.
We're gonna need it, and we just-- to have something.
But we sold that one too.
And I remember this-- also that German woman bought.
But I don't know if she bought it for herself or-- or she sold it.
I don't know.
Anyway, so from-- and then I-- I worked in the-- in-- in the shop.
And then my father got-- got-- this was already in-- in '42.
My father got sick.
And [SIGHS] there were no doctors.
There was just like a-- like a-- what they call a [NON-ENGLISH], you know, like now you could say in-- like a nurse-- nurse's helper, or whatever-- something like this.
So he came to see what's going on with my father.
And he had a high temperature.
And he just stopped eating.
And my father was very tall and a very strong, strong man.
And he just dwindled down to nothing.
He just stayed in bed.
He didn't want to get up anymore, and just stayed there.
And--
